Title: Hoppers
Author: Unknown
Summary: “Hoppers” (2026) is a PG-rated animated sci-fi comedy directed by Daniel Chong, set to release on March 6, 2026. Produced by Walt Disney, it stars Jon Hamm, Bobby Moynihan, and Piper Curda. The film follows Mabel as she explores a world where human consciousness can be transferred into robotic animals to uncover hidden mysteries.
Content:
Hoppers (2026) is an action animated comedy sci-fi film rated PG, with a runtime of 105 minutes. It is set to be released on March 6, 2026. The film is directed by Daniel Chong and written by Jesse Andrews and Daniel Chong. The cast includes Jon Hamm, Bobby Moynihan, and Piper Curda. It is produced by Walt Disney Pictures.

In “Hoppers,” scientists have discovered how to “hop” human consciousness into lifelike robotic animals, allowing people to communicate with animals as animals. Using this new technology, Mabel (Curda) will uncover mysteries within the animal world that are beyond anything she could have imagined.

Title: Sirāt
Author: Unknown
Summary: "Sirāt," an R-rated foreign suspense drama directed by Oliver Laxe, releases in Los Angeles and New York on November 14, 2025, and nationwide on March 6, 2026. The 115-minute film stars Sergi López and others, following a father and son searching for their missing daughter at a Moroccan rave amid electronic music and raw freedom.
Content:
Sirāt is a drama film set in a foreign suspense genre, rated R, with a runtime of 115 minutes. It is scheduled for release on November 14, 2025, in Los Angeles and New York, and on March 6, 2026. Directed by Oliver Laxe and written by Santiago Fillol and Oliver Laxe, the film stars Sergi López, Bruno Núñez, Stefania Gadda, Joshua Liam Henderson, Tonin Janvier, Jade Oukid, and Richard Bellamy. 

The story follows a father and his son who arrive at a rave deep in the mountains of southern Morocco. They are searching for Mar, their daughter and sister, who vanished months earlier at one of these endless, sleepless parties. Surrounded by electronic music and a raw, unfamiliar sense of freedom, they repeatedly show her photo, clinging to hope as they follow a group of ravers to one last destination.

Title: The Bride
Author: Unknown
Summary: "The Bride" (2026), directed by Maggie Gyllenhaal, is an R-rated horror drama romance releasing on March 6, 2026. Featuring stars like Jessie Buckley, Christian Bale, and Annette Bening, it tells the story of a lonely Frankenstein who revives a murdered woman to create a companion in 1930s Chicago. The film explores macabre romance and ambitious themes.
Content:
The Bride (2026) is a horror drama romance film rated R, with a runtime of 126 minutes. It is set to be released on March 6, 2026. The film is directed and written by Maggie Gyllenhaal, known for her work on The Lost Daughter. The cast includes Jessie Buckley, Christian Bale, Peter Sarsgaard, Annette Bening, Jake Gyllenhaal, and Penélope Cruz. It is produced by Warner Brothers.

The story follows a lonely Frankenstein (Bale) who travels to 1930s Chicago to meet groundbreaking scientist Dr. Euphronious (Annette Bening) to create a companion. They revive a murdered young woman, and The Bride (Buckley) is born.

The film is described as a bold, iconoclastic take on a compelling story, filled with life, macabre romance, and ambitious ideas. Maggie Gyllenhaal's vision results in a film that is both beautiful and hideous, a creature difficult to describe.

Title: Traffic Collison - Circle Dr E/Highway 11 - Update
Author: Unknown
Summary: Saskatoon police report that one eastbound lane on Circle Drive and one northbound lane on Highway 11 are open after a semi-trailer struck a bridge, causing significant damage. No injuries occurred. Traffic restrictions remain, and motorists are advised to use alternate routes as engineers assess the damage. The collision is under investigation.
Content:
The Saskatoon Police Service advises that city engineers have assessed the damage, and one eastbound lane on Circle Drive and one northbound lane of Highway 11 have been opened for traffic to proceed. The Collision Analyst Unit, in cooperation with the Saskatchewan Highway Patrol, is investigating the collision. No injuries were reported.

Traffic flow restrictions will remain until further notice. Motorists are asked to take alternate routes or expect delays in the area.

A semi with trailer carrying a piece of equipment in the northbound lane of Highway 11, entering the city limits at the Circle Drive overpass, was too high for the structure and caused significant damage. As a result, the northbound lanes of Highway 11 into the city and the eastbound lanes of Circle Drive over the bridge are closed until engineers from the City of Saskatoon assess the damage and determine if it is safe for travel. Motorists are advised to take alternate routes until further notice.

Title: The Inertia v3 Beta is Here
Author: Paul Redmond
Summary: The Inertia v3 Beta introduces a built-in XHR HTTP client, making Axios optional, and adds a use Httphook for external HTTP requests. It features optimistic updates across router and forms, supports concurrent updates, and offers instant navigation for smoother user experience. Additionally, a server-side preserve Fragment option maintains URL hashes during redirects.
Content:
The Inertia v3 Beta is a major pre-release milestone that introduces several new features and improvements. It now includes a built-in XHR HTTP client, making Axios an optional peer dependency. Applications that do not require Axios can omit it entirely, while those that prefer it can continue using it by installing it as a peer dependency.

A new use Httphook allows making HTTP requests outside of Inertia, providing the same developer experience as use Form— including processing, errors, progress, and dirtystate—but returns response data instead of triggering page navigation. It supports surfacing all validation errors, not just the first.

Optimistic updates have been added across the router, use Form, and use Http, including support for concurrent optimistic updates. The update callback can receive current props and return the optimistic state to apply immediately, with automatic rollback on validation errors, server errors, or interrupted requests. The router supports both a fluent API and an inline option for optimistic updates.

The use Form hook now works similarly, allowing optimistic updates when adding new items. The <Form> component also gains an optimistic prop for declarative handling.

Instant support has been added to make navigation feel immediate by rendering the destination page before the server response completes.

A new server-side preserve Fragment option keeps the URL hash fragment intact across redirects. When the server includes preserve Fragment: true in the Inertia page object, the client retains the original request's URL fragment even during redirects.

Title: Human Strategy In An AI-Accelerated Workflow
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Carrie Webster)
Summary: AI is transforming UX design by automating tasks like wireframes and prototypes, raising fears of job replacement. While AI accelerates artifact creation, UX remains focused on navigating ambiguity, advocating for users, and solving complex problems. The industry is adjusting to AI’s capabilities, but core principles of human-centered design continue to define the field.
Content:
UX design is entering a new phase, with designers shifting from makers of outputs to directors of intent. AI can now generate wireframes, prototypes, and even design systems in minutes, but UX has never been only about creating interfaces. It’s about navigating ambiguity, advocating for humans in systems optimized for efficiency, and solving their problems through thoughtful design.

I’ve been working in User Experience design for more than twenty years. Long enough to have held many job titles, from when stakeholders asked us to “just make it pretty” to when wireframes were delivered as annotated PDFs. Over the years, many tools and methodologies have come and gone, and entire platforms have disappeared. Yet, nothing has unsettled designers quite like AI.

When generative AI tools first entered my workflow, my reaction wasn’t excitement — it was unease, with a bit of curiosity. Watching an interface appear in seconds, complete with sensible spacing, readable typography, and halfway-decent copy, triggered a very real fear: if a machine can do this, where does that leave me?

That fear is now widespread. Designers at every level ask the same question, often quietly: “Will an AI agent replace me by next week, month, or year?” The timeline varies depending on where you are in your career and how quickly your employer chooses to adopt AI tools. I have been fortunate in some roles to work with organizations that haven’t allowed the use of AI due to data security concerns. Discussions about AI’s impact are ongoing on platforms like Reddit.

Fearing the takeover of AI in our roles is not irrational. We’re seeing AI generate wireframes, prototypes, personas, usability summaries, accessibility suggestions, and entire design systems. Tasks that once took days can now be completed in minutes.

Here’s the uncomfortable truth: If your role is largely about producing artifacts, drawing buttons, aligning components, or translating instructions into screens, then parts of that work are already being automated. Still, UX design has never been solely about creating a user interface. It’s about navigating ambiguity, advocating for humans within systems optimized for efficiency, and translating complex needs into effective solutions.

Title: Crude prices make record jump as Trump’s measures fail to calm markets
Author: James Bikales
Summary: Crude oil prices surged past $90 per barrel amid ongoing disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz, as U.S. efforts to stabilize markets failed. Iran’s damage to tankers and production cuts by Iraq and Kuwait have worsened supply issues, with analysts warning prices could reach $200. The U.S. has implemented measures like sanctions easing and naval escorts, but supply disruptions persist.
Content:
Crude prices made a record jump as Trump’s measures failed to calm markets. The Trump administration has yet to solve the fundamental issue of getting oil and gas tankers moving through the Strait of Hormuz again, analysts said.

A week after it began its strikes on Iran, the Trump administration’s efforts to stem the rise in energy prices have yet to turn the tide — and analysts warn the worst of the price shocks may still be to come.

The U.S. benchmark oil price eclipsed $90 a barrel on Friday for the first time since 2023, up more than $20 since the war began Saturday, marking the market’s highest one-week jump in history. The price increase has already started to affect American consumers, with prices at the gas pump up 32 cents a gallon from a week ago.

The unyielding price increases are attributed to the continued disruption in the Strait of Hormuz, through which 20 percent of the world’s crude passes each day. This comes as President Donald Trump faces rising pressure to contain the economic impact of the war on Americans, eight months ahead of the midterms, where affordability issues are a top concern.

“Crude WILL go to $200 [a barrel], en route higher, unless traffic through the Strait resumes,” said Rory Johnston, an oil analyst who writes the newsletter Commodity Context. “Not clickbait, but rather brutal physics and necessary economic incentives.”

The White House announced several measures aimed at calming the oil markets this week, including temporarily easing sanctions on India’s purchase of Russian oil and offering naval escorts and political risk insurance to oil and gas tankers traversing the Strait of Hormuz. However, none of these measures has succeeded, as traders boost prices on news of supply disruptions.

Iran has succeeded in damaging several oil tankers, while Iraq and Kuwait have already throttled oil production because their crude tankers can no longer reach markets. China has warned it could stop exporting fuel amid supply concerns.

The longer ships avoid the Strait of Hormuz, the more Gulf countries will start to run out of storage capacity and be forced to shut in their production, said Claudio Galimberti, chief economist at Rystad Energy. “If the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for, let’s say, three weeks, then you will have shut in 15 million barrels a day of production in the Middle East,” Galimberti said. “That takes us from a position of comfortable oversupply as of last Friday to one of incredible deficit, the size of which we’ve never seen before.”

Galimberti also noted that oil may be slow to flow even if shipping traffic resumes, given the difficult and expensive processes required to restart production. “If it’s shut for weeks or months, then it’s going to take weeks and probably longer to get production back to normal.”

Title: King, Domani Strike Back at 50 Cent with Two Angry Diss Tracks
Author: Trent Fitzgerald
Summary: King and Domani released diss tracks, "Droptop" and "PU$Y," attacking 50 Cent for disrespecting T.I. and the Harris family on the Power Origins theme song. 50 Cent responded with provocative posts, including a photoshopped image, escalating the feud. The conflict centers on lyrical disses and personal insults related to the show's theme song.
Content:
King and Domani released two diss tracks on their Instagram pages in retaliation against 50 Cent for disrespecting T.I. and the Harris family on the new Power Origins theme song.

King's diss track is called "Droptop." On the song, he criticizes 50 Cent for talking too much smack and accuses him of allegedly beating women. "You were made from 50 Cent because ya mama gave it cheap," King raps.

Meanwhile, Domani delivered his diss track titled "PU$Y." On the haunting song, he challenges 50 Cent's tough-guy persona. "I smell p***y / How about you come here and try to push me / That suga water got ya, now you turn sweet, huh / I smell p***y / Ni**a, I read you like a book read / Come bring ya a*s to school quit playin' hookie / I smell p***y," he raps.

Both songs are responses to 50 Cent, who featured T.I. and the Harris family on the theme song for his upcoming television show, Power Origins. On the track, which features R&B singer Leon Thomas, 50 raps about the drug trade: "I'm back on my dope boy grammar / Your daddy made your mama eat every box in Atlanta," and "Freak sh*t / Peep sh*t / Keep sh*t, on the low / But everybody know."

50 Cent captioned his Instagram post about the song, "50cent Ft Leon Thomas (No one told us what we’re here for) POWER ORIGINS theme song!"

He further escalated tensions by posting a photoshopped image of T.I. with Tiny eating a piece from a cut-up cardboard box, possibly implying that 50's lyric was directed at T.I. and the Harris family matriarch. "LOL...They are gonna learn to leave me alone," 50 Cent wrote in the caption.

The feud between 50 Cent and the Harris family is intensifying.

Title: Weekly Chartstopper: March 6, 2026
Author: Michael Normyle
Summary: Since Iran's conflict began, oil prices have surged 30-35%, impacting global energy markets and raising inflation expectations. U.S. GDP could decline by 0.6%, with markets reducing Fed rate cut forecasts by 20 basis points. Despite economic concerns, the Nasdaq-100 fell only 1%, while the 10-year Treasury yield increased to 4.15%. Key upcoming data include CPI, PCE, and GDP revisions.
Content:
US Markets Weekly Chartstopper: March 6, 2026

March 06, 2026 — 05:00 pm EST

Since the conflict with Iran began on Saturday, markets have been trying to assess its economic impact, primarily through higher energy prices. The conflict has effectively halted traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, which transports 20-25% of global oil and liquefied natural gas (LNG), and has shuttered energy facilities in the region.

This week, international and U.S. oil prices have risen 30% and 35%, respectively, reaching their highest levels since 2023. European LNG prices are up 65%, while U.S. LNG prices have increased by 10%. JPMorgan estimates that if oil prices remain at these levels, U.S. headline inflation could rise by 0.3 percentage points, and U.S. GDP growth could decrease by 0.6 percentage points. 

Given the likely boost to inflation, markets have reduced their expectations for Federal Reserve rate cuts this year by 20 basis points to around 40 basis points. This adjustment follows a significant jobs report miss, with the economy losing 92,000 jobs in February—against expectations of a 55,000 gain—and broad-based losses across sectors. However, it’s important not to overinterpret a single month’s data, as the private sector has averaged a gain of about 20,000 jobs per month over the past three months.

Despite this, the Nasdaq-100® is down just 1% this week, though rising inflation expectations have pushed the 10-year Treasury yield up nearly 20 basis points to 4.15%.

Next Week

Here are the top events to watch:

Tuesday: NFIB Small Business Optimism (Feb.)  
Wednesday: CPI Inflation (Feb.)  
Thursday: Jobless Claims  
Friday: PCE Inflation and Spending (Jan.), Real GDP (Q4 revision), JOLTS Job Openings (Jan.)

Title: U.S. says it worked with Ecuador to bomb alleged drug-trafficking operations site
Author: Unknown
Summary: The U.S. and Ecuador conducted a joint operation targeting a drug traffickers' camp near the Colombian border in Ecuador, involving helicopters, aircraft, boats, and drones. The camp belonged to Colombian FARC dissidents. Ecuador’s President Noboa prioritizes cracking down on organized crime, with the U.S. described as a key partner. No details on casualties were provided.
Content:
The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a joint operation targeting drug trafficking in Ecuador, authorities in both countries announced on Friday. The U.S. described the operation as "lethal kinetic operations." Neither U.S. Southern Command nor Ecuador's defense ministry provided details on whether anyone was killed or captured during the strike.

The operation involved helicopters, aircraft, river boats, and drones to locate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in northeastern Ecuador near the Colombian border, according to Ecuador's defense ministry. The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian criminal organization composed of FARC dissidents, with a capacity for 50 people.

Ecuadorian President Daniel Noboa has prioritized a military crackdown on organized crime. His government has also imposed tariffs on Colombia, accusing it of insufficient efforts to combat drug trafficking. Noboa is scheduled to attend the "Shield of the Americas" summit in Miami this weekend, where regional leaders will discuss security and organized crime.

The Ecuadorian defense ministry stated that the United States is a key ally in this fight. "At the request of Ecuador, the Department of War executed targeted action to advance our shared objective of dismantling narco-terrorist networks," said Pentagon spokesman Sean Parnell. This operation followed a similar U.S.-Ecuador effort announced earlier this week by U.S. Southern Command.

Title: Nintendo sues the US government for a refund on tariffs
Author: Amanda Silberling
Summary: Nintendo filed a lawsuit against the U.S. government seeking a refund for tariffs paid under President Trump’s executive orders, which were struck down by the Supreme Court for exceeding presidential authority. Over a thousand companies are also pursuing refunds on tariffs totaling over $200 billion. Trump increased tariffs from 10% to 15%, prompting 24 states to sue.
Content:
Nintendo filed a lawsuit against the U.S. government on Friday seeking a refund for tariffs it paid due to President Donald Trump’s executive orders invoking the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA). The lawsuit, filed in the U.S. Court of International Trade, follows a Supreme Court decision that struck down the tariffs imposed under IEEPA, ruling that the president exceeded his authority. More than a thousand other companies have also sued for refunds on these tariffs, which have resulted in over $200 billion collected on imports in total.

Nintendo confirmed the filing, stating, "We have nothing else to share on the topic." In response to the Supreme Court’s decision, President Trump raised tariffs from 10% to 15%. Subsequently, 24 states have sued, arguing that the president has once again overstepped his powers by making this change.

Title: X is testing a new ad format that connects posts with products
Author: Sarah Perez
Summary: X is testing a new ad format that inserts product recommendations beneath posts referencing the company or its products, initially seen with Starlink in Europe. The suggested links direct users to product websites, and the feature aims to provide non-intrusive advertising. This follows recent initiatives like "Paid Partnership" labels and creator monetization tools, as X seeks to attract more marketers and compete with larger social platforms.
Content:
X is testing a new ad format that inserts a recommendation directly underneath a post that references the company or its products. The initial test, spotted by an X user in Europe, displayed a suggestion to “Get Starlink” beneath a post from a user that said Starlink’s satellite service works great in Portugal. The link, when clicked, directed users to Starlink’s website. X head of product Nikita Bier confirmed the test, responding, “Trying to make an ad product that isn’t an ad.”

The Starlink ad is not visible to all users at this time, but the placeholder where the ad sits is. If a user posts from @levelsio’s account on March 6, there will be an outlined box beneath the text of the post. This box currently showcases a random X post unless the user is in a location where the ad test is live. 

In places where the ad displays, several commenters noticed the new addition, with one asking, “lmao, did you add this Starlink button?” In response to a suggestion that X should allow affiliate links in this ad slot, Bier said, “No, then people will lie. I want to trust recommendations on here.” 

The test follows earlier news that the company is rolling out “Paid Partnership” labels for creators. These labels can be applied to posts to comply with social media advertising regulations, instead of requiring creators to use hashtags like “ad” or “paid partnership.” If sponsored posts were combined with embedded links like the one being tested, X could potentially attract more marketers to the platform. This could encourage creators to use the app more, helping it compete against larger social networks such as Instagram, YouTube, and TikTok.

X has been pursuing creator content for some time—long before it was called “X” and before Elon Musk’s ownership. The platform has introduced various creator products, including payouts for viral content, ad-revenue sharing, and creator subscriptions. Recently, the company revamped its Creator Subscriptions offering with new features, including the ability to monetize individual threads.

Additionally, X announced that its integrated chatbot Grok can now read X’s long-form content, known as Articles. However, this feature remains underutilized, as many creators prefer to publish lengthy written content through their own websites or newsletters.

Title: CyHi Addresses G.O.O.D. Music Breakup on "The G.O.O.D. Ole Days"
Author: Trent Fitzgerald
Summary: Cy Hi addresses the breakup of G.O.O.D. Music in his song "The G.O.O.D. Ole Days," blaming strained relationships with Travis Scott, Kid Cudi, and Kanye West. He cites a lack of collaboration, differing loyalties, and Ye's difficult leadership as factors. Cy Hi also announces a new album, "The Story of Mr. EGOT," coming soon.
Content:
Cy Hi addresses Travis Scott, Kid Cudi, and the reasons behind the G.O.O.D. Music breakup on his new song, "The G.O.O.D. Ole Days." On Friday (March 6), Cy Hi released his new song where he discusses artists from Ye's G.O.O.D. Music label and why the breakup happened.

Produced by Mark Byrd and Phil Blackman, the song features Cy Hi explaining his fallout with G.O.O.D. Music members Travis Scott, Kid Cudi, and others. According to Cy Hi, his friendship with Travis deteriorated when he couldn't get a verse from him in exchange for co-writing Travis's biggest hit, "Sicko Mode." He raps, "I like what he did in fashion, but ni**a just look hella tacky. When I helped him with his album, it went like [11-times] platinum, but I still ain't got my swap and he mad that I'm even askin'."

Regarding Kid Cudi, Cy Hi believes their relationship soured because Cudi sided with Ye during his past disagreement with Cudi. He raps, "It could have got real ugly / They said that Ye almost f**k around and killed buddy, and that's why their relationship is still muddy. And I'm caught between it all. Man, ni**a should feel for me."

As for Ye, Cy Hi acknowledges he was also a reason for G.O.O.D. Music's dissolution but empathizes with the Chicago rapper-producer, noting Ye's leadership efforts. He lyrically explains, "Okay, I understand that. But you know sometimes Kanye could be a little difficult. I get it, but I understand him in a different way. Y'all just know the beautiful dark twisty Ye. He's just an honest person, but it gets conveyed as somebody with a broken heart against the 808. I ain't going to lie. Sometimes it gets in the business way. He just ain't afraid to say what you didn't say."

Near the end of the song, Cy Hi announces he's dropping a new album titled "The Story of Mr. EGOT" "real soon."

Title: Buy the Spike in CrowdStrike or Okta Stock After Posting Record Profitability?
Author: Zacks
Summary: CrowdStrike and Okta outperformed expectations in Q4, posting record profitability and strong revenue growth amid market volatility. CrowdStrike’s $1.3 billion sales and 47% ARR growth reflect surging demand for AI-driven cybersecurity. Okta’s $761 million sales and expanding enterprise adoption highlight platform consolidation success. Both stocks surged over 10% post-earnings, with positive full-year guidance.
Content:
Technology

Buy the Spike in CrowdStrike or Okta Stock After Posting Record Profitability?

In a week marked by broader market volatility, cybersecurity firms CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Okta (OKTA) have emerged as outperformers after exceeding their Q4 expectations. Both stocks have surged more than 10% since reporting their Q4 results on Tuesday and Wednesday, respectively. They delivered standout Q4 reports, showing record profitability, strong revenue growth, expanding recurring revenue bases, and rising demand for cybersecurity and identity solutions.

CrowdStrike’s AI-Driven Security Demand

CrowdStrike’s strong Q1 results were defined by faster-than-expected growth amid surging demand for its Falcon cybersecurity platform as AI-driven cyber threats become more sophisticated. Notably, Falcon is a cloud-native cybersecurity solution that provides advanced endpoint detection and response (EDR), managed detection and response (MDR), and next-generation antivirus capabilities to protect organizations from cyber threats.

Falcon’s growth led to CrowdStrike posting Q4 sales of $1.3 billion, up 24% from a year ago and surpassing estimates of $1.29 billion. Its net new annual recurring revenue (ARR) grew 47% year over year, reaching a quarterly record of $331 million. More importantly, CrowdStrike saw record profitability since going public in 2019, with Q4 EPS rising 9% to a peak of $1.12, surpassing expectations of $1.10.

Okta’s Enterprise Adoption & Platform Consolidation

Okta’s standout Q4 report was highlighted by steady subscription growth, expanding enterprise adoption, and strong cash flow, signaling a successful transition for its unified identity security platform. The consolidated platform allows customers to integrate with nearly any application, service, or cloud through a secure, reliable, and scalable security system. Accelerating adoption of Okta's platform was attributed to trust from large organizations as they modernize their security architecture.

With solid revenue growth, Okta’s Q4 sales increased 11% to $761 million, beating estimates of $749.1 million. Its remaining performance obligations (RPO) rose 15% year over year, while current RPO grew 12%, indicating strong future revenue. Okta also posted record profitability, with Q4 EPS rising 15% to $0.90, beating expectations of $0.85. Other highlights included Q4 free cash flow increasing 11% to a record $252 million.

Full-Year Results & Guidance

Capping off a record year, CrowdStrike’s annual sales surged 22% to $4.81 billion, while full-year EPS skyrocketed to $3.73 from previous levels.

Title: Par Petroleum (PARR) Q4 Earnings: How Key Metrics Compare to Wall Street Estimates
Author: Zacks
Summary: Par Petroleum reported Q4 2025 revenue of $1.81 billion, up 5.9% from estimates, and EPS of $1.17, beating revenue expectations but slightly below EPS estimates. Key refinery throughput metrics exceeded analyst estimates, while some production costs were higher than expected. Overall, the company showed mixed performance relative to Wall Street forecasts.
Content:
Technology Par Petroleum (PARR) Q4 Earnings: How Key Metrics Compare to Wall Street Estimates

For the quarter ended December 2025, Par Petroleum (PARR) reported revenue of $1.81 billion, down 1% over the same period last year. EPS came in at $1.17, compared to -$0.79 in the year-ago quarter. The reported revenue represents a surprise of +5.9% over the Zacks Consensus Estimate of $1.71 billion. With the consensus EPS estimate being $1.21, the EPS surprise was -2.91%.

While investors closely watch year-over-year changes in headline numbers—revenue and earnings—and how they compare to Wall Street expectations to determine their next course of action, some key metrics always provide a better insight into a company's underlying performance. Since these metrics play a crucial role in driving the top- and bottom-line numbers, comparing them with the year-ago numbers and what analysts estimated about them helps investors better project a stock's price performance.

Here is how Par Petroleum performed in the just reported quarter in terms of the metrics most widely monitored and projected by Wall Street analysts:

Total Refining - Feedstocks Throughput: 190,900 thousands of barrels of oil versus the three-analyst average estimate of 189,129.2 thousands of barrels of oil.

Hawaii Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 87.10 MMBBL/D compared to the 85.96 MMBBL/D average estimate based on three analysts.

Washington Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 37.00 MMBBL/D versus 36.08 MMBBL/D estimated by three analysts on average.

Wyoming Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 14.40 MMBBL/D compared to the 15.53 MMBBL/D average estimate based on three analysts.

Montana Refinery - Feedstocks Throughput: 52.40 MMBBL/D compared to the 51.55 MMBBL/D average estimate based on three analysts.

Adjusted Gross Margin per barrel - Washington Refinery: $8.32 versus $6.39 estimated by two analysts on average.

Production costs per barrel - Wyoming Refinery: $13.27 versus the two-analyst average estimate of $10.25.

Production costs per barrel - Washington Refinery: $4.57 compared to the $4.08 average estimate based on two analysts.

Production costs per barrel - Hawaii Refinery: $4.15 versus the two-analyst average estimate of $4.70.

Revenues from Refining: $1.75 billion versus the two-analyst average estimate of $1.57 billion. The reported number represents a year-over-year change of -1.2%.

Revenues from Retail: $142.28 million compared to the $145.87 million average estimate based on two analysts. The reported number represents a change of +0.5% year over year.

Revenues from Logistics: $73.71 million versus $74 million estimated by two analysts on average.

Title: Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian apologizes to Gulf states as war enters second week
Author: Anya Zoledziowski
Summary: After Iran’s supreme leader was killed in a U.S.-Israel strike, Iran retaliated against Israel and U.S. bases. Over 1,230 deaths have occurred, including 217 in Lebanon, amid escalating conflict and humanitarian crises. Iran targeted Israel and U.S. installations, while Israel intensified strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon. Canada is evacuating citizens; Trump called for Iran’s unconditional surrender.
Content:
Days after Iran’s supreme leader was killed in a U.S.-Israel strike, Iran retaliated against Israel and U.S. bases as Israel intensified strikes on Iran-backed Hezbollah in Lebanon.

The U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has continued to escalate after nearly a week, resulting in over 1,230 deaths in Iran, with the Lebanese Health Ministry reporting 217 fatalities and more than 790 wounded due to Israeli attacks. The situation has led to a humanitarian crisis, with thousands seeking evacuation.

Iran launched retaliatory attacks on Israel and U.S. military installations, while Israel increased its campaign against Iran-backed Hezbollah militants in Lebanon. On Friday, U.S. President Donald Trump stated that the only deal with Iran to potentially end the war would be "unconditional surrender."

Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian issued an apology to Gulf states affected by the conflict but rejected Trump's demand for Iran's surrender. Prime Minister Mark Carney announced that Canada had no plans to engage militarily, focusing instead on evacuating 3,500 Canadians who requested assistance.

The conflict has seen widespread destruction, with Israeli jets striking Tehran and Iran targeting Kuwait. Smoke rises over southern Beirut following Israeli strikes, and sirens blare over Tel Aviv as Israel's defense systems engage.

Title: Getting Started With The Popover API
Author: hello@smashingmagazine.com (Godstime Aburu)
Summary: The article explains the evolution of tooltips in web development, highlighting the new Popover API as a native browser solution. Traditionally, tooltips required complex JavaScript and faced accessibility issues. The Popover API aims to simplify implementation, improve accessibility, and reduce reliance on external libraries, with broad browser support enhancing native tooltip handling.
Content:
The article discusses the evolution of tooltips in web development, focusing on the new Popover API as a native browser solution. Traditionally, building tooltips involved using JavaScript libraries, which often led to complex, hard-to-maintain code. Although tooltips seem simple—appearing on hover or focus and disappearing when the user moves away—implementing them correctly for all users, including those relying on keyboard navigation and screen readers, proved challenging.

Common issues included flickering, overlapping content on small screens, and accessibility problems such as screen readers announcing tooltips incorrectly or not at all. Over time, the developer's tooltip code grew increasingly complex, with multiple event listeners, manual ARIA attribute management, and handling outside clicks, making maintenance difficult. While libraries provided some relief, they often acted as black boxes, obscuring the underlying behavior.

This prompted exploration of the newer Popover API, which aims to handle these interactions natively within the browser. Although still evolving and with some parts in flux, the API currently enjoys broad support across major browsers. Using the Popover API can simplify tooltip implementation, making them more accessible and reducing reliance on external libraries.

Title: February 2026 Review and Outlook
Author: The Market Intelligence Desk Team
Summary: In February 2026, U.S. equities showed mixed results amid sector rotation. Large-cap indices were restrained, with the Nasdaq 100 down 2.3%, while equal-weight and mid-cap indices outperformed. Market leadership shifted toward cyclicals and defensives like energy and healthcare. Treasury yields declined, and economic data indicated a cooling economy. Overall, the month reflected ongoing rotation rather than a risk-off environment.
Content:
US Markets February 2026 Review and Outlook

The Market Intelligence Desk Team

Market Intelligence Desk by this author ->

Executive Summary: U.S. equities delivered a mixed headline performance amid significant internal rotation. Market leadership broadened as equal-weight, mid-cap, and value exposures outperformed. Market breadth improved with seven of 11 large-cap sectors within 0.5% of their respective 52-week highs. Mega-cap growth and AI-adjacent software weighed on cap-weighted indices. Treasury yields declined meaningfully, led by the long end. Energy prices strengthened amid geopolitical risk, reinforcing real-asset leadership. Earnings growth remains solid despite increasing dispersion beneath the surface.

U.S. equity markets delivered a mixed performance in February, with headline indices masking significant rotation and dispersion beneath the surface. Among large caps, the Dow Jones Industrials rose modestly by 0.3%, the S&P 500 declined 0.8% for the second time in three months, and the Nasdaq 100 fell 2.3%, posting its worst monthly decline since March 2025. Cap-weighted indices were restrained by corrective price action in select large-cap growth stocks, as seen in the Magnificent Seven Index, which declined 7.3%, its worst since March 2025. Conversely, the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index gained 3.5%, its best since May 2025, outperforming the cap-weighted S&P 500 for the fourth consecutive month. The S&P Midcap 400 rose 4.1%, its best month since May 2025.

Importantly, February did not resemble a broad risk-off environment. Instead, market action was characterized by rotation rather than liquidation. Investors continued reallocating away from concentrated leadership toward a broader mix of cyclicals and defensives, with strong gains across energy, utilities, materials, staples, industrials, and healthcare. This shift suggests confidence in the durability of economic activity, even as debate persists around the return profile and timing of large-scale AI investments.

From a macro perspective, the backdrop remained supportive but nuanced. Economic data indicated a gradually cooling economy rather than contraction, while inflation progress remained uneven. Treasury yields declined significantly during the month, with the 10-year yield falling below 4%, reinforcing expectations for a patient Federal Reserve. Overall, February appeared less like a turning point for equities and more like an extension of an ongoing transition toward broader participation and more selective leadership.

The divergence in February was largely driven by continued weakness in mega-cap growth, particularly within technology.
